Context: Define and distinguish intramarket (calendar) spreads and intermarket spreads, and give one concrete example of each as described in the text. Explain the main risk rationale for using spreads.
answer: Intramarket (calendar) spread: long one delivery month and short another delivery month of the same futures market (e.g., long March Treasury bonds, short June Treasury bonds of the same year). Intermarket spread: long one market and short a related market (e.g., long S&P 500 and short Russell 2000 futures). Rationale: by holding long and short positions in related instruments, large directional moves tend to affect both legs similarly, so one gains and the other loses, reducing net directional risk.

Context: In precious metals, what are contango and backwardation, and how do changes in interest rates and the price of the metal affect the spread between delivery months?
answer: Contango is the normal positive-carry term structure in which deferred futures trade above nearby; backwardation is negative carry where nearby trade above deferred. In precious metals, higher interest rates (with unchanged spot) widen the spread; higher metal prices (with unchanged rates) also widen the spread (interest is charged on a higher contract value). Declines in either interest rates or the metal price narrow the spread.
